message:
perf(btree): pool StackEntry cell_pointers Vec across page loads

Every fresh `load_page` allocated a `Vec<u16>` for the cell-pointer
offsets and every pop/drop of that `StackEntry` freed it. On the MT 8t
mt-mvcc-bench profile `read_cell_pointers_into` was 1.55 % self-time
and the allocator helpers (`_int_malloc` 2.18 %, `RawVecInner::finish_grow`
0.62 %, `cfree` 0.71 %) compounded across hot operations that load many
pages per step (`table_seek_for_insert` 0.94 %, `move_to_rightmost_leaf`
0.69 %). The cursor stack is bounded at depth 20 and most cursor ops
load 2–4 pages, so a small thread-local free list of those `Vec<u16>`
buffers covers the steady-state working set.

Add a thread-local `CELL_POINTERS_POOL: Vec<Vec<u16>>` capped at 32
entries / 8192 u16 capacity per buffer:

  thread_local! {
      static CELL_POINTERS_POOL: RefCell<Vec<Vec<u16>>> = ...;
  }
  fn take_pooled_cell_pointers() -> Vec<u16> { ... }
  fn recycle_cell_pointers(mut buf: Vec<u16>) { ... }

`StackEntry` switches from `#[derive(Clone)]` to a manual `Clone` that
pulls a buffer from the pool and `extend_from_slice`s the source bytes
in, plus a manual `Drop` that returns the field's allocation to the
pool via `mem::take`. `RightmostLeafCacheEntry` (the only other
`Vec<u16>`-bearing entry on the cursor's hot path) gets a sibling `Drop`
so its buffers also flow through the pool when the cache slot is
replaced or cleared.

`load_page` and `reload_page_fresh` route through the pool by calling
`take_pooled_cell_pointers()` and feeding the resulting buffer into
`cell::read_cell_pointers_into`, which already supports caller-owned
storage. After warmup the same allocations cycle indefinitely between
"on a stack entry" and "in the pool" — `_int_malloc` / `cfree` traffic
on the cursor descent path is bounded by depth-once-per-thread instead
of per-page-load.

Bound the pool by both buffer count (32 — generous for stacks of depth
≤ 20 plus a few cached/cloned entries) and per-buffer capacity (8192
u16 slots — enough for a 64 KiB page fully populated with 4-byte cells,
above which we drop the allocation back to the system instead of
keeping a pathologically large buffer pinned forever).

Add `cell_pointers_pool_recycles_buffer_capacity` to lock in the
recycle/take contract: a recycled buffer comes back at the same heap
address with capacity preserved and length cleared, empty buffers are
silently dropped, and oversized buffers (> `CELL_POINTERS_POOL_MAX_CAPACITY`)
are released to the allocator instead of pooled.

Pure performance change — `cell_pointers` semantics are identical, only
the allocator interaction is rerouted.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
